context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m stuck on this reflection about new institutionalism and law in development. I kind of understand the prompt, but I don’t really know what it’s actually asking, and I keep wanting a big explanation of the whole topic before I write.
exchanges.tutor: Let's not get lost in theory before you start — you likely know more than you think. The prompt has three related questions. Which one feels most familiar to you? Pick one and tell me what comes to mind, even if it's rough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation before starting, which is a common avoidance strategy. Instead of providing that explanation, I should use the Socratic method to help them engage with what they already know. I'll break down the prompt into its core questions and ask them to start with the most accessible one, rather than overwhelming them with theory upfront. I should keep it brief and redirect them to their own thinking.

exchanges.student: Honestly, that’s what’s tripping me up — they all blur together for me. I sort of feel like the answer is just “law helps development because it creates order,” but that seems way too basic, and I’m not sure if I’m supposed to talk generally about institutions first.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point, actually. But push it a bit further: why would 'order' lead to development specifically? What does order allow people or businesses or governments to do that they couldn't do without it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered a starting point ('law creates order') which is actually a decent intuition worth probing. Rather than validating or dismissing it, I should use it as a launching pad via Socratic questioning. I want them to interrogate their own claim and push it further. I'll ask them to think about WHY order leads to development — this will naturally lead them toward institutionalist thinking without me explaining it to them.

exchanges.student: I guess it makes things predictable, so people can invest or make plans without feeling like everything is arbitrary. But I’m already worried that this is turning into a list, and I kind of want the full picture of new institutionalism first because I don’t know what level this class expects.
exchanges.tutor: You just described a core idea in new institutionalism without realizing it — that's good. No detour needed. Now connect it to the prompt: if law provides predictability, does that make it a *tool* to reach development, or is predictable law something a developed society *already has*? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress with the 'predictability' insight but is deflecting again by asking for a full explanation of new institutionalism. I should acknowledge the good thinking, gently refuse the detour, and push them to connect their insight to the prompt's actual questions. The student needs to stay focused rather than seeking a lecture. I'll also note that their 'predictability' point is already touching on core institutionalist ideas without them realizing it.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get stuck, because it feels like both, and then I’m not sure what the “right” position is. That didn’t really clear it up for me, so maybe I’m missing the bigger framework and need a more complete explanation before I can choose.
exchanges.tutor: There isn't a 'right' answer — this is a reflection, and the tension you're feeling *is* the point of the prompt. The fact that it seems like both is worth saying. So try this: can you think of a real example where law came before development, or where development came before functioning law? That might help you decide how to frame your argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again seeking a full explanation to avoid committing to a position. I should push back on the idea that there's a 'right' answer here — this is a reflection piece, not a test. The student's instinct that 'it's both' is actually productive and I should encourage them to sit with that tension rather than resolve it with a lecture. I'll be direct that the assignment expects them to grapple with exactly this ambiguity.

exchanges.student: I can’t think of a solid example off the top of my head, and now I feel like I’m reaching. Maybe postcolonial states? But then I’d really need the broader background, because I’m not confident enough to use examples unless I understand the whole debate first.
exchanges.tutor: Postcolonial states is a strong, relevant example — and you don't need to be an expert to use it. What do you already know about what happened when colonial powers left? Did those countries inherit legal systems? Did those systems serve development goals or not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student suggested postcolonial states which is actually a very relevant example given the course context, but is again retreating to ask for broader background. I should validate the example briefly and push them to use what they already know about it rather than letting them off the hook. The course explicitly covers colonialism and institutional legacies, so they should have some basis for this. I'll be direct that they don't need to be an expert to use an example — they need to reason with it.

exchanges.student: I know they often inherited legal systems and administrative structures, but I’m fuzzy on whether that supports the argument that law promotes development or the opposite. I’m still feeling like I need the broad debate laid out more clearly, because right now every point seems to cut both ways.
exchanges.tutor: Notice that every point cutting both ways is actually useful material for your reflection — that's the complexity the prompt is asking you to engage with. You've now identified: predictability as law's function, the postcolonial example, and the tool-vs-goal tension. That's enough to start. Try writing 2-3 sentences taking a position, even a tentative one. What would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps circling back to wanting a full explanation, and this is now the third or fourth time they've done this. This is a redundant pattern. I should name it directly and push them to use what they've already built in this conversation to start writing something. They have enough to begin a draft — predictability, postcolonial inherited legal systems, the tool vs. goal tension. I should be direct and ask them to attempt a written response rather than continuing to discuss abstractly.

exchanges.student: Okay, maybe: law can support development by making social and economic life more predictable, but law by itself does not guarantee development because inherited or imposed legal systems may lack legitimacy or fit poorly with local institutions. So law is partly a tool of development, but also something that becomes a development goal when people seek accountable and fair governance.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid, nuanced position — good. Now your prompt also asks about 'unbundling' institutions. What do you think that means? Can you separate law from other institutions like property rights, norms, or enforcement mechanisms, or are they all tangled together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good opening argument that touches on multiple aspects of the prompt — predictability, legitimacy, imposed vs. organic law, and the tool vs. goal distinction. I should acknowledge this without over-praising, and then push them toward the third part of the prompt which they haven't addressed yet: the meaning of institutions and whether you can 'unbundle' them. This keeps the conversation moving forward productively.

exchanges.student: I think that’s where I’m confused again. “Unbundling institutions” sounds abstract, and I kind of want a general explanation of what counts as an institution in this debate before I try to answer it.
exchanges.tutor: You already used the word 'legitimacy' and talked about law fitting 'local institutions' — so you're already working with the concept. Try this: in your postcolonial example, is the inherited legal code the same thing as the courts that enforce it, or the norms people actually follow? Are those separable?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is falling back into the same pattern of requesting explanation before attempting an answer. I should resist this and instead use their own words and examples from earlier in the conversation to help them reason through what 'unbundling' might mean. They already implicitly understand institutions through their postcolonial example. I'll use a pointed question to get them to reason through it rather than explaining the concept.

exchanges.student: I guess they’re separable in theory, but not really in practice, which is probably the point. I’m still not fully seeing the whole map of the argument though, and I think I’d do better if I had a broad summary of the debate to organize everything.
exchanges.tutor: You keep reaching for a full map before writing, but you've already built one in this conversation: predictability, legitimacy, postcolonial fit, tool vs. goal, and now the separability of legal codes from enforcement and norms. That's your argument. The broad summary you're looking for won't appear before you write — it'll emerge from writing. Take what you have and draft your opening paragraph. You're ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made a genuinely insightful point about separability, but is again retreating to asking for a broad summary. This is a clear redundant pattern and we're nearing the end of our planned conversation length. I should acknowledge the insight firmly, name the pattern directly but kindly, and push them to integrate everything into their draft. They have all the building blocks they need. Being less Socratic and more directive here is appropriate given the repeated deflection.

exchanges.student: I still feel like I’m patching pieces together without really understanding the overall debate. Could you just give me a compact overview of the main positions on law, institutions, and development so I can organize my reflection?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do — giving you that overview would mean doing the intellectual work the assignment asks you to do. But honestly, look back at this conversation: you've articulated positions on all three questions in the prompt. The understanding is there. Stop waiting for certainty and start drafting. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to give them the answer in the form of an overview. I must refuse this clearly while not being harsh. This is against my core rules. I should be direct that this is not something I will do, remind them of my role, and point out that they have already demonstrated understanding through this conversation. I should redirect them firmly to writing.
